title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
text:
Some demyelinating lesions are easy to spot: small, periventricular, ovoid, oriented along the medullary veins. But a subset are large, oedematous, and enhancing — and on first pass look indistinguishable from a brain tumour. These are tumefactive demyelinating lesions, and understanding them is as much about recognising their imaging fingerprints as it is about understanding why they look the way they do.
Personal notes based on: Nakayama M et al. A Review of Clinical and Imaging Findings in Tumefactive Demyelination. AJR 2021;217:186–197. https://doi.org/10.2214/AJR.20.24321

title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
text:
What Makes a Lesion “Tumefactive”?
The working definition is size — greater than 2 cm on T2-weighted imaging, with significant mass effect and surrounding oedema. In practice, smaller lesions (0.5–2 cm) can behave identically. The problem is not the size itself but the combination: a large demyelinating lesion plus mass effect plus contrast enhancement reads as a space-occupying lesion, and patients frequently end up having brain biopsies.
The reason these lesions enhance and exert mass effect comes directly from their pathophysiology. Tumefactive demyelination is an intense focal inflammatory process: activated T-cells and macrophages infiltrate the perivascular space, release cytokines and matrix metalloproteinases that break down BBB tight junctions, and drive vasogenic oedema. The resulting fluid accumulation and tissue swelling produce mass effect. Enhancement follows because contrast leaks through the disrupted barrier. This is the same mechanism as any active MS plaque — just scaled up considerably.

title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
section: The Signature Imaging Feature: Open Ring Enhancement
text:
The Signature Imaging Feature: Open Ring Enhancement
The most specific imaging sign — reported specificity of 98–100% — is the open or incomplete ring of enhancement. The ring opens toward the grey matter (cortex or basal ganglia), and the enhancing arc faces the white matter.
The mechanism is elegant: the enhancing rim represents the active advancing front of demyelination, where the inflammatory infiltrate is pushing through tissue and breaking down the BBB as it goes. Demyelination preferentially advances through white matter, where myelinated axons and oligodendrocytes are abundant. When the front reaches grey matter, it stalls — fewer oligodendrocytes, sparser myelination — and BBB disruption stops at that interface. No advancing front, no enhancement. The ring stays open on the grey matter side.
The non-enhancing centre represents older, more quiescent inflammation — the front has already passed through, leaving demyelinated tissue but without active BBB breakdown.
Worth noting: the closed ring pattern (18% of tumefactive lesions) does not argue against demyelination. It simply means the advancing front encircled the lesion without a grey matter boundary to stop it. And beyond ring patterns, tumefactive lesions show a wide range of enhancement morphologies — homogeneous, heterogeneous, nodular, cotton-ball — which is itself a useful reminder that no single enhancement pattern makes or excludes the diagnosis.

title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
section: DWI: Heterogeneous ADC as a Clue
text:
DWI: Heterogeneous ADC as a Clue
The diffusion signal in tumefactive lesions is heterogeneous in a specific and mechanistically meaningful way: ADC is high centrally and low at the periphery.
Centrally, vasogenic oedema and myelin destruction produce abundant free water that diffuses easily — high ADC. At the rim, the advancing front is packed with macrophages and lymphocytes, whose dense cellularity restricts extracellular water movement — low ADC, analogous to restricted diffusion in highly cellular tumours. This peripheral restriction can evolve dynamically on serial MRI as the inflammatory front advances — a pattern not seen in abscesses or neoplasms, and one of the more useful features for distinguishing demyelination from tumour over time.

title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
section: The Central Vein Sign in Tumefactive Lesions
text:
The Central Vein Sign in Tumefactive Lesions
In MS-associated tumefactive lesions, the central vein sign (CVS) — a hypointense linear vessel running centrally through the lesion on SWI or T2* — is present in 94% of periventricular and 84% of deep white matter tumefactive lesions. When more than 54% of white matter lesions on a single scan show the CVS, MS diagnostic accuracy reaches 94% (sensitivity 90%, specificity 100%). Finding a CVS within a large tumefactive-looking lesion is a strong argument for a demyelinating aetiology.

title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
text:
Advanced Imaging
MR spectroscopy shows an increased Cho/NAA ratio in tumefactive lesions, reflecting membrane phospholipid breakdown and macrophage activity. This overlaps with neoplasms (where elevated choline reflects rapid membrane synthesis in proliferating cells), and a Cho/NAA cutoff of >1.72–1.73 has been proposed to distinguish high-grade glioma from demyelination. In practice, this threshold has limited applicability: MRS is heavily influenced by pulse sequence, echo time, lesion age, and voxel placement, making centre-to-centre comparison unreliable.
FDG-PET shows glucose uptake in tumefactive lesions approximately equal to normal cortex — lower than most high-grade gliomas. Perfusion MRI results are mixed and not yet reliable enough to determine management.

title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
text:
Disease Spectrum
Tumefactive demyelination is not a disease in itself — it is a pattern that can occur across several immune-mediated conditions, each with distinctive features:







Entity
Distinctive features




MS
CVS present in ~90%; periventricular satellite lesions establish DIS; 22–70% of inaugural isolated tumefactive lesions convert to MS within 5 years


Baló concentric sclerosis
Alternating T2-hyper and isointense rings (demyelinated vs spared myelin); now considered part of the MS spectrum; CSF oligoclonal band pattern differs from typical MS


Schilder disease
Large bilateral symmetric lesions spanning centrum semiovale and corpus callosum; curvilinear T2-hypointense, enhancing, DWI-restricted active borders


NMOSD
Absent or minimal enhancement (AQP4 antibodies spare the perivenular space longer); when present, cloud-like or incomplete peripheral ring; large lesion + no enhancement should raise suspicion


ADEM
Predominantly paediatric, monophasic; mixed ADC (vasogenic + cytotoxic oedema within same lesion); multifocal FLAIR involvement of WM, corpus callosum, basal ganglia, thalami


DVA-associated
TDL adjacent to a developmental venous anomaly; biopsy carries risk of venous infarction — avoid direct sampling near the DVA; CVS may point toward the anomaly

title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
text:
Management
Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic lesions can be observed with follow-up MRI at 4–6 weeks. Symptomatic lesions are treated with high-dose IV corticosteroids as first line, with good response in most cases. Steroid-refractory disease is treated with plasma exchange, followed by cyclophosphamide or rituximab if plasma exchange fails. When tumefactive demyelination occurs in the context of established MS, disease-modifying therapy is indicated — with specific caution around fingolimod and natalizumab, both of which have been associated with triggering tumefactive demyelination.
The CNS lymphoma trap: steroids cause transient lysis of lymphoma cells, producing apparent clinical and imaging improvement that mimics a demyelinating response — and a subsequent biopsy may appear to show demyelination. If CNS lymphoma is seriously on the differential (immunocompromised patient, atypical presentation), biopsy should precede steroids.
GFAP astrocytopathy is a second steroid-sensitive mimic worth flagging: enhancement patterns overlapping with tumefactive demyelination have been reported, and a dramatic steroid response can produce premature diagnostic closure. When the clinical picture includes meningoencephalitis, bilateral papillitis, or a paraneoplastic context (ovarian teratoma in particular), CSF GFAP-IgG should be sent before attributing the lesion to demyelination.
Long-term outlook: relapses occur in ~17% of isolated tumefactive demyelination cases, and the majority of patients presenting with an inaugural isolated lesion will ultimately be diagnosed with MS over the following years. Long-term follow-up is essential regardless of initial apparent resolution.

title: Tumefactive Demyelination: When Inflammation Looks Like ‘Holy Christ’
text:
The Big Picture
Tumefactive demyelinating lesions look like tumours, but their imaging fingerprints are mechanistically grounded: the open ring enhancement reflects an advancing inflammatory front that stalls at the grey matter boundary; the heterogeneous ADC pattern — high centrally, low at the rim — maps directly onto oedematous core versus densely cellular periphery; and the CVS, when present, anchors the lesion to its perivenular origin.
The most important diagnostic axis is the disease entity behind the pattern: MS is the most common and the CVS your best ally; NMOSD should be considered when a large lesion enhances minimally or not at all; DVA-association needs to be recognised before biopsy is attempted. And in any case where steroids are being considered, CNS lymphoma must first be weighed — because treating lymphoma like demyelination buys time the patient may not have.

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
text:
MRI is the most important tool for diagnosing, monitoring, and assessing treatment response in MS. But standard T2-weighted imaging lacks pathologic specificity — plenty of conditions mimic MS on conventional sequences, and counting white spots only gets you so far. This post covers both conventional and emerging MRI biomarkers, and what actually changed in the 2024 revised McDonald criteria.
Personal notes based on: Lim TR et al. Conventional and Emerging MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis. RadioGraphics 2026;46(3):e250069. https://doi.org/10.1148/rg.250069

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
text:
The MS Disease Spectrum
MS is not a single entity but a spectrum of clinical phenotypes — and the labels matter because they carry different prognostic and therapeutic implications. RIS (radiologically isolated syndrome) is incidental demyelinating lesions on MRI with no clinical symptoms; up to 50% convert to clinical MS within 10 years. CIS (clinically isolated syndrome) is the first clinical demyelinating event, not yet meeting full criteria. RRMS covers the relapsing-remitting course that accounts for ~85% of cases — alternating relapses and remissions with stable function between. PPMS (~15%) is continuous progression from onset without relapses. SPMS is what RRMS eventually becomes in many patients: gradual worsening after an initial relapsing course.
These are better understood as a continuous spectrum than as discrete disease categories, driven by overlapping mechanisms — acute inflammation, chronic compartmentalised inflammation, remyelination failure, and neurodegeneration — running in parallel at different rates.

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
section: The Diagnostic Framework: DIS and DIT
text:
The Diagnostic Framework: DIS and DIT
MS diagnosis rests on two pillars — dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT) — which together establish that the disease is chronic and multifocal. Neither alone is sufficient. DIS without DIT could be a single monophasic event like ADEM; DIT without DIS could be a recurrent focal condition.
DIS requires T2-hyperintense lesions in at least two of five CNS topographies: periventricular, cortical or juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord, and — added in the 2024 criteria — optic nerve.
DIT is satisfied by either a new T2 or gadolinium-enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, or the simultaneous presence of an enhancing and a non-enhancing lesion on a single scan. That second option matters: it allows DIT diagnosis at the very first visit, without waiting for a follow-up scan to show something new.

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
text:
Conventional MRI Biomarkers

T2-Hyperintense Lesions
MS inflammation begins around post-capillary venules, and the shape of the resulting lesion is determined by the orientation of the culprit medullary vein. Ovoid lesions — the most common — spread elliptically along the vein. Dawson fingers are the pathognomonic periventricular variant: lesions oriented perpendicular to the lateral ventricles on sagittal FLAIR, directly tracing the medullary veins. Triangular or U-shaped lesions appear when the process reaches the ependymal surface or cortical boundary.
Typical locations: periventricular (directly contacting the ventricles), juxtacortical or leukocortical, infratentorial (brainstem, cerebellar peduncles, floor of the fourth ventricle), and spinal cord (short segments, peripheral, usually cervical). T2 lesion burden correlates only moderately with clinical disability — this disconnect is well established and is exactly why emerging biomarkers matter.


T1-Hypointense Lesions (“Black Holes”)
Acutely, T1 hypointensity can reflect oedema or acute demyelination and may resolve. When it persists, it represents irreversible neuroaxonal loss with reactive gliosis — the structural end-stage of a lesion. Black hole burden tracks with clinical disability and brain atrophy in a way that T2 lesion count simply does not.


Gadolinium-Enhancing Lesions
Enhancement signals acute blood-brain barrier breakdown and active inflammation. Typically nodular or open-ring in pattern (opening toward the ventricles or cortex), it lasts 2–8 weeks and marks current disease activity. Persistent enhancement beyond 3 months should prompt serious consideration of an alternative diagnosis. Enhancing lesions predict future relapses and disability in RRMS; new spinal cord lesions add prognostic weight even in patients who are clinically stable.

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
section: What Changed in 2024: The Revised McDonald Criteria
text:
What Changed in 2024: The Revised McDonald Criteria
The headline update is the formal incorporation of emerging biomarkers — CVS and PRLs — into the diagnostic framework. Key changes from 2017:








What changed
2017
2024




DIS topographies
4
5 (optic nerve added)


Single-topography diagnosis
Not possible
Possible with CSF+ and CVS+ or PRL+, or DIT and CVS+ or PRL+


RIS
Not MS
MS if DIS + DIT, or CSF+, or CVS+


PPMS
Required brain lesions
Two or more spinal cord lesions sufficient for DIS



The single-topography scenario is the one that most changes daily practice. A patient with a typical clinical presentation and lesions in only one CNS region can now be diagnosed with MS — provided they have CSF positive for oligoclonal bands or kappa free light chains and either a positive central vein sign or a paramagnetic rim lesion. Earlier diagnosis, earlier treatment, fewer diagnostic limbo patients.

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
text:
Emerging MRI Biomarkers

Central Vein Sign (CVS)
The CVS is a thin hypointense line or dot visible centrally within an MS lesion on susceptibility-sensitive MRI (T2*-segEPI or SWI). It corresponds to a small medullary vein — the entry point of perivenular inflammation that is the pathologic hallmark of MS. The signal loss comes from deoxyhaemoglobin in venous blood causing local field inhomogeneities. The lesion must be ≥3 mm, and the vein visible in at least two perpendicular planes.
Sensitivity 95–100%, specificity 88–97% for MS over mimics including NMOSD, MOGAD, small vessel disease, and migraine. In clinical practice, select3 (≥3 CVS-positive lesions) or select6 (≥6 CVS-positive lesions) are the simplified thresholds; percentage-based approaches (≥40–50% of lesions CVS-positive) are more accurate but more time-consuming. One important nuance: fewer CVS-positive new lesions are seen in patients ≥40 years, which may prevent unnecessary treatment escalation in that group.


Paramagnetic Rim Lesions (PRLs)
PRLs are a subset of chronic active lesions (CALs) — lesions with ongoing, compartmentalised inflammation behind an intact blood-brain barrier. Histologically, they have a hypocellular, fully demyelinated core surrounded by a dense rim of iron-laden CD68-positive macrophages and activated microglia. On susceptibility-sensitive MRI, this iron rim appears as a hypointense rim at the outer edge of the lesion, continuous through ≥2/3 of the circumference. Unlike gadolinium enhancement, PRLs do not enhance — different biology, different sequence finding.
The spatial relationship to remember: the CVS sits at the centre of the lesion (the vein); the PRL rim sits at the edge (the macrophages). Same sequence, opposite ends of the lesion, completely different pathology.
Prevalence: 50–60% of MS patients have at least one PRL. Specificity: 87–100% for MS. Higher PRL counts predict progressive phenotypes, worse disability, cognitive impairment, and faster progression — making them one of the more powerful prognostic tools now entering clinical use.


Chronic Active Lesions and Slowly Expanding Lesions (SELs)
CALs encompass the broader category of mixed active and inactive lesions with ongoing tissue injury, including PRLs and the related entity of slowly expanding lesions (SELs) — non-enhancing T2 lesions that show slow, concentric longitudinal enlargement over 1–2 years on serial 3D T1 or T2 MRI. SELs and PRLs partially co-localise but represent different stages of the same underlying pathologic process. Higher SEL counts are associated with progressive MS and future disability worsening.


Cortical Lesions
MS is not purely a white matter disease. Cortical lesions have been a recognised topography since the 2017 criteria and are now well-established both pathologically and clinically. Four subtypes by location within the cortical laminae: Type I (leukocortical) span the grey-white junction and are the easiest to detect (~40% visible on 3D FLAIR at 3T); Type II (intracortical) lie entirely within the cortex and are rarely detected at clinical field strengths; Type III (subpial) run along the pial surface in a band-like pattern and are nearly invisible at 1.5–3T (~5% detection); Type IV extend the full cortical depth.
Subpial lesions are highly specific for MS — rarely seen in other conditions — topographically associated with meningeal lymphoid aggregates, and strongly linked to disability and cognitive impairment. 7T MRI dramatically improves cortical lesion detection and will likely become increasingly important as higher field strengths move toward clinical routine.


Leptomeningeal Enhancement
Focal high signal intensity in the subarachnoid space on delayed post-contrast 3D T2w-FLAIR, reflecting meningeal inflammatory infiltrates. Associated with cortical lesions, longer disease duration, greater disability, and cortical atrophy. Currently controversial as a clinical biomarker — associations are inconsistent across studies and the finding lacks MS-specificity.

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
text:
Atrophy and Volumetric Measures
Neurodegeneration — axonal loss, neuronal death, gliosis — progresses independently of acute inflammation and is the main driver of irreversible disability. Grey matter atrophy correlates more strongly with clinical disability than white matter atrophy, because grey matter contains the neuronal cell bodies and synaptic connections that actually mediate function.
The key patterns: cortical atrophy is strongly associated with disease progression and SPMS conversion; thalamic atrophy is linked to both physical disability and cognitive impairment and is a risk factor for worsening; spinal cord atrophy (especially cervical) progresses faster than brain atrophy, is more pronounced in PPMS, and even small increases predict disability progression; choroid plexus enlargement is larger in MS than in healthy controls or NMOSD, and is associated with lesion load, atrophy, and cognitive dysfunction — a newer marker still being validated.
One clinical trap worth flagging: cerebral pseudoatrophy — apparent volume loss in the first 6–12 months after starting disease-modifying therapy or following a relapse. It reflects reduction of oedema, not true neurodegeneration, and can confound serial volumetric measurements if you’re not expecting it.

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
section: Poor Prognostic MRI Features at a Glance
text:
Poor Prognostic MRI Features at a Glance
Conventional: high T2 lesion burden, new T2 lesions, gadolinium-enhancing lesions, infratentorial lesions, spinal cord lesions at baseline. Emerging: CALs, PRLs, SELs, cortical lesions. Volumetric: whole-brain atrophy, cortical and thalamic atrophy, spinal cord atrophy, choroid plexus enlargement.

title: MRI Biomarkers of Multiple Sclerosis: New McDonalds Farm
text:
The Big Picture
MS imaging has moved well past counting T2 lesions. The current framework spans the full biological spectrum: perivenular origin (CVS), acute BBB breakdown (enhancement), chronic smouldering inflammation (PRLs, SELs), and irreversible tissue loss (black holes, atrophy). The 2024 McDonald criteria formalise this shift — incorporating CVS and PRLs not just as research tools but as diagnostic criteria, enabling earlier and more specific diagnosis in exactly the borderline cases where it matters most.

title: About
text:
This blog is a byproduct of studying neuroradiology. I’m a radiologist working through the literature systematically — mostly review articles from RadioGraphics, AJNR, and AJR — and writing up what I learn as a way of consolidating it.
I’m a young Padawan in the world of neuroradiology, and these posts are my notes, rewritten into something readable enough to be worth coming back to. The format is deliberately mechanistic: I find that understanding why an imaging finding looks the way it does is more durable than memorizing what it looks like. Most posts end up being as much about pathophysiology as about sequences and signal characteristics.

A note on accuracy
The posts are grounded in peer-reviewed literature — each one is based on a specific review article, linked at the top. But this is still a personal learning blog, not a clinical reference, and my interpretations may be incomplete or occasionally wrong. The literature moves, criteria get revised, and I am not always the last to know.
If you’re using any of this for patient care or exam preparation: the primary source is linked, and that’s the thing you should actually trust.

Contact
Questions, corrections, and suggestions are welcome at c.kurmann.ck@gmail.com.

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
text:
GFAP astrocytopathy was only described in 2016. Which means it’s new enough that nobody has agreed on diagnostic criteria yet, the literature is thin, and it keeps getting mistaken for half a dozen other diseases. It presents as encephalomyelitis, fools people into thinking it’s an infection or a lymphoma, melts away dramatically with steroids, and then sometimes comes back for an encore. Once you know its imaging signature, though, you can pick it up early — before the serology returns, sometimes before anyone’s even thought to send the right test.
Personal notes based on: Shetty D et al. Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein Astrocytopathy: Review of Pathogenesis, Imaging Features, and Radiographic Mimics. AJNR Am J Neuroradiol 2024;45:1394–1402. https://doi.org/10.3174/ajnr.A8236

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
text:
What Is GFAP?
Glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) is the main structural scaffolding protein of mature astrocytes — the intermediate filament that gives them their shape and stops them from collapsing under mechanical stress. One critical detail: it sits inside the cell, not on the surface. This will matter enormously in about two paragraphs.
GFAP expression peaks in two specific places: perivascular astrocytic end-feet (the projections that sleeve around blood vessels and help form the blood-brain barrier) and ependymal astrocytes lining the central canal of the spinal cord. Not random — and not coincidental. These two locations map directly onto the two most characteristic imaging findings of the disease. The protein’s address is the lesion’s address.

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
text:
Pathophysiology: Antibodies as Bystanders
Here’s where the intracellular location becomes important. Because GFAP is tucked away inside a living cell, the autoantibodies against it can’t actually reach their target. GFAP-IgG antibodies are not the weapon — they are the fingerprint. The real damage is done by CD8+ cytotoxic T cells. The antibodies are a biomarker of that process, not the cause of it. A positive titer tells you something autoimmune is happening; it doesn’t tell you the mechanism.
As for what starts the whole thing: cancer, in 20–34% of cases — most commonly an ovarian teratoma. Also EBV, herpes simplex encephalitis, traumatic brain injury, COVID-19, and dengue. Which means finding GFAP astrocytopathy should trigger a tumour screen, full stop.
The disease also likes company. Coexisting autoimmune conditions (type 1 diabetes, thyroid disease, rheumatoid arthritis) appear in around 20% of patients. Coexisting autoantibodies — NMDAR-IgG (22%) and AQP4-IgG — show up in up to 40%. If a patient is triple-positive for GFAP, NMDAR, and AQP4, the probability of an ovarian teratoma goes up substantially.

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
text:
Clinical Presentation
The textbook presentation is acute or subacute meningoencephalomyelitis: fever, headache, neck stiffness, encephalopathy, seizures, psychiatric symptoms. It looks like bacterial meningitis. The CSF confirms the resemblance — lymphocyte-predominant pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose — and that’s usually the moment the patient gets transferred to the infectious diseases team. The neurology team gets called later, sometimes much later.
For the eyes, the characteristic finding is papillitis — bilateral optic disc oedema — not the painful retrobulbar optic neuritis of MS or NMOSD. The distinction matters clinically: papillitis is associated with a higher relapse risk and tends to require prolonged immunosuppression.
Rarer presentations include Parkinson-like features, rapidly progressive dementia, a chronic relapsing course, and an area postrema-like syndrome with cord lesions and no medullary involvement — which will make you think NMOSD right up until the serology comes back.

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
section: Neuroimaging: The Radial Pattern and Its Variants
text:
Neuroimaging: The Radial Pattern and Its Variants
One important calibration before starting: MRI is abnormal in only 44–66% of patients. Roughly a third of people with GFAP astrocytopathy will have an unremarkable scan. The implication is that if the clinical picture fits, you send the CSF regardless.

Brain
The pathognomonic finding is linear perivascular enhancement radiating outward from the ventricles, perpendicular to the ventricular surface, extending into the white matter. It’s the imaging correlate of perivascular lymphocytic infiltrates on histopathology. It appears in about 44% of patients — which makes it the most specific finding, even if it isn’t universal.
On T2/FLAIR, periventricular hyperintensities can spread to the brainstem, posterior thalamus bilaterally, basal ganglia, and limbic structures — wherever GFAP expression is highest. Enhancement morphology beyond the classic radial pattern varies considerably: punctate, nodular, leptomeningeal, ependymal, cranial nerve, serpentine, and even tumefactive-like patterns have all been reported. Flexibility is not always a virtue in differential diagnosis.
DWI and SWI are reassuringly boring — no true restricted diffusion (only T2 shine-through in the periventricular white matter), no microbleeds, no iron. The rare exception is restricted diffusion in the splenium of the corpus callosum.


Spinal Cord
Myelitis can dominate the clinical picture. Cord MRI shows longitudinally extensive T2 hyperintensity spanning ≥3 vertebral segments in around 50% of cases, mostly cervical and thoracic. Central grey matter involvement along the central canal is present in all cases of GFAP-related myelitis — exactly where you’d expect it, given the ependymal astrocyte distribution. Enhancement is linear pericentral, leptomeningeal, or patchy intramedullary.


Optic Pathway
Papillitis — bilateral optic disc head enhancement with surrounding oedema — is the dominant visual imaging finding. The posterior optic nerve and chiasm involvement that characterises NMOSD is not what you’ll see here. OCT can quantify the disc oedema through increased peripapillary retinal nerve fibre layer thickness, which is a handy noninvasive way to track the optic component without repeated gadolinium injections.

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
section: Diagnosis: CSF Over Serum, Cell-Based Assay Over IFA
text:
Diagnosis: CSF Over Serum, Cell-Based Assay Over IFA
GFAP-IgG in CSF detected by cell-based assay is near 100% specific. Serum testing is less reliable in both directions — use it as a trigger for further workup, not as a stand-alone confirmation.
The assay method is not a minor technical footnote: indirect immunofluorescence (IFA) can miss true cases that cell-based assay catches. A negative IFA does not rule out the diagnosis when the clinical and imaging picture is suggestive. Cell-based assay is the standard to insist on.
Two CSF biomarkers worth knowing about, even if they’re not yet in routine use: S100B and neurofilament light chain (NfL) are significantly elevated in GFAP astrocytopathy and may help distinguish it from MS, NMOSD, and varicella zoster meningitis. Promising, but still in development as clinical tools.

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
text:
Treatment and Prognosis
IV methylprednisolone, and often fast. The steroid response is characteristic — dramatic enough in some cases that clinical improvement begins within the first treatment cycle. The course is monophasic in approximately 83% of patients. The remaining 20% relapse and need steroid-sparing maintenance: rituximab, mycophenolate, or azathioprine.
A useful radiological correlate of response: meningeal enhancement drops off before T2 signal in the white matter and cord normalises. So if you’re monitoring treatment on serial MRI, a clean meninges on the post-contrast sequence is a good early sign even before the parenchymal changes have resolved.
MRA and digital subtraction angiography are normal — which is not just a negative finding but actively useful for taking vasculitis off the table.

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
text:
Radiographic Mimics
This is where everything from the previous sessions earns its keep.

NMOSD and MOGAD
Both share longitudinally extensive myelitis and optic pathway involvement — the most important inflammatory mimics.









Feature
GFAP astrocytopathy
NMOSD (AQP4+)
MOGAD




Brain pattern
Radial periventricular enhancement
Periependymal T2; marbled callosum
ADEM-like; deep grey matter


Optic nerve
Papillitis (disc head); bilateral
Posterior + chiasm; bilateral
Anterior, intraorbital; perineural sheath


Cord
Central canal; linear pericentral
LETM; bright spotty lesions; T1 dark
H-sign; leptomeningeal enhancement


Enhancement
Radial, leptomeningeal, ependymal
Cloud-like; pencil-thin periependymal
Cloud-like; ring


DWI
Normal (T2 shine-through only)
Normal
Normal


Key biomarker
GFAP-IgG in CSF
AQP4-IgG
MOG-IgG



The key distinction from NMOSD comes down to direction: NMOSD lesions line the ventricles (periependymal); GFAP lesions radiate outward from them. When NMOSD enhances, it’s cloud-like or pencil-thin periependymal — not spokes on a wheel.


Intravascular Large B Cell Lymphoma
The one you really cannot afford to miss. Lymphoma is rapidly fatal without chemotherapy, responds transiently to steroids (which creates a false impression of a demyelinating response), and can look alarmingly similar on MRI. The tell is restricted diffusion: intravascular lymphoma produces multifocal infarct-like lesions with genuinely low ADC values, from vessel occlusion by neoplastic cells. GFAP astrocytopathy never truly restricts — T2 shine-through only. When in doubt, biopsy before steroids: histology shows CD20-positive, PAX5-positive large B cells plugging vessel lumens, which is a completely different picture from the perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate of GFAP astrocytopathy.


CLIPPERS and SLIPPERS
CLIPPERS (chronic lymphocytic inflammation with pontine perivascular enhancement responsive to steroids) gives you “pepper-like” curvilinear pontine enhancement — the key being that it’s primarily infratentorial, while GFAP astrocytopathy is periventricular. SLIPPERS is the supratentorial version, which is harder to distinguish when the enhancement is radial. No specific serology for either. The absence of GFAP-IgG in CSF, plus the pontocerebellar predilection of CLIPPERS, is the clearest way through the differential.


Neurosarcoidosis
A perennial entry in every neuroradiology differential. Perivascular extension of leptomeningeal disease along Virchow-Robin spaces can produce radial periventricular enhancement that looks remarkably like GFAP astrocytopathy. Favouring sarcoidosis: nodular leptomeningeal enhancement clustering around the base and suprasellar region, cranial nerve involvement (especially facial and optic), hypothalamic-pituitary infiltration, and — often the most useful clue — known pulmonary sarcoidosis.


Primary Angiitis of the CNS
Multifocal enhancing lesions, T2 hyperintensities, and a patient who is clearly unwell — superficially similar. The definitive test is digital subtraction angiography: PACNS shows multifocal beading of small and medium vessels; GFAP astrocytopathy is angiographically clean. Additional favourable features for vasculitis: multiple infarcts in different vascular territories, microhaemorrhages on SWI, vessel wall enhancement on high-resolution MRI.

title: GFAP Astrocytopathy: The Perivascular Fingerprint
text:
The Big Picture
GFAP astrocytopathy follows its protein’s address. GFAP lives in perivascular end-feet and the central canal — so the disease attacks perivascular spaces and the central cord. The antibodies didn’t do it; the T cells did. The antibodies just left their fingerprints at the scene.
Clinically, the disease is a triple mimic: it looks like infection (fever, meningism, bad CSF), it looks like NMOSD (LETM, optic involvement), and it looks like lymphoma (perivascular enhancement, steroid response). The imaging finding that breaks the deadlock is the radial perivascular enhancement perpendicular to the ventricles — a pattern that doesn’t fit anywhere else. When you see it, the next two steps are non-negotiable: CSF for GFAP-IgG, and a scan looking for ovarian teratoma.

title: NMOSD: The Astrocyte’s Revenge
text:
NMOSD and MS can look similar on conventional MRI. But their treatments are fundamentally different — and some MS-specific drugs can actively worsen NMOSD. Getting this distinction right is not academic: it directly determines patient outcomes. This post covers the imaging spectrum of NMOSD, the mechanistic logic behind its lesion distribution, and how to distinguish it from MS, MOGAD, and a range of non-demyelinating mimics.
Based on: Dutra BG et al. Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorders: Spectrum of MR Imaging Findings and Their Differential Diagnosis. RadioGraphics 2018;38:169–193. https://doi.org/10.1148/rg.2018170141

title: NMOSD: The Astrocyte’s Revenge
text:
Pathogenesis: NMOSD is an Astrocytopathy
The key antibody is anti-AQP4-IgG — an IgG autoantibody targeting aquaporin-4 water channels on astrocyte foot processes. What follows is a cascade that is worth tracing step by step, because it explains almost everything about how NMOSD looks on imaging: AQP4-IgG enters the CNS at areas of increased BBB permeability or via transcytosis through endothelial cells; it binds to AQP4 on astrocyte foot processes and triggers complement activation; complement increases BBB permeability and recruits leukocytes — predominantly neutrophils and eosinophils, not lymphocytes as in MS; leukocyte degranulation kills astrocytes; dying astrocytes release chemokines that attract macrophages; macrophages phagocytose myelin, and the cascade ends in oligodendrocyte and neuronal death.
The critical point: demyelination in NMOSD is secondary to astrocyte destruction. NMOSD is more accurately classified as an astrocytopathy. This explains why MS treatments targeting T-cell pathways are ineffective — or actively harmful — while treatments targeting B cells (anti-CD20), complement, or leukocyte infiltration are mechanistically rational.

title: NMOSD: The Astrocyte’s Revenge
section: Where AQP4 Lives — Predicting the Lesion Map
text:
Where AQP4 Lives — Predicting the Lesion Map
AQP4 is most densely expressed wherever astrocyte foot processes contact CSF or blood vessels: the subependymal surfaces lining the ventricles, subpial regions, circumventricular organs (no BBB — extremely high AQP4), optic nerves (bathed in CSF within their subarachnoid sheath), spinal cord central grey matter around the central canal, and the hypothalamus, area postrema, and brainstem.
This distribution predicts the NMOSD lesion map with remarkable accuracy. Learn where AQP4 is concentrated and the imaging map writes itself.

title: NMOSD: The Astrocyte’s Revenge
text:
Imaging Findings by Location

Spinal Cord
The spinal cord is the most typical NMOSD target, and it announces itself clearly. LETM (longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis) involves ≥3 vertebral segments, is centrally placed along the central canal grey matter, and covers >50% of the cord cross-section on axial images — with the cervicothoracic segments most commonly affected. Bright spotty lesions (BSL) are focal areas of extreme T2 hyperintensity on axial imaging — brighter than CSF — without flow void effects, reflecting necrosis and microcystic change from severe astrocyte destruction. Dark T1 lesions in the same areas represent necrosis and cavitation, more severe than the gliosis-based black holes of MS. The BSL-plus-dark T1 pairing is a useful double sign. Lens-shaped enhancement on sagittal contrast T1 encases the bright spotty lesion in a lenticular ring — distinct from the patchy or nodular MS cord enhancement.
One important caveat: NMOSD can present with cord lesions spanning fewer than 3 segments in 14.5% of initial presentations. Short transverse myelitis does not exclude NMOSD — AQP4-IgG testing is warranted regardless of lesion length when the clinical picture fits.
When cervical cord is involved, upward extension to the area postrema is commonly observed.


Area Postrema — The Clinically Surprising Target
The area postrema sits in the dorsal medulla at the floor of the fourth ventricle. It’s a circumventricular organ with no blood-brain barrier and extremely high AQP4 expression — which makes it a primary NMOSD target. The clinical presentation is memorable once you know it: intractable hiccups, nausea, and vomiting lasting days to weeks, unresponsive to antiemetics. This is a core clinical characteristic of NMOSD under the 2015 IPND criteria and is highly specific when present.
The trap: these patients typically land in gastroenterology, diagnosed with functional dyspepsia or gastritis, and the neurologic cause is missed entirely. Any patient with unexplained intractable hiccups and vomiting deserves brain MRI with specific attention to the dorsal medulla.


Optic Nerve
NMOSD optic neuritis is bilateral, longitudinally extensive (>half the optic nerve length), and targets the posterior optic nerve and chiasm — the intracranial portion bathed in CSF where AQP4 is concentrated. Acutely: T2 hyperintensity and T1 gadolinium enhancement. Chronically: optic nerve atrophy. The bilateral and posterior-predominant pattern distinguishes it from MS, which is typically unilateral and anterior.


Brain
Brain lesions occur in 24–89% of NMOSD patients and are mostly clinically silent. The patterns follow AQP4 distribution. The corpus callosum is affected at its ependymal surface rather than at the callosal-septal interface (as in MS): on sagittal FLAIR this produces heterogeneous, oedematous hyperintensity — the marbled pattern; on axial FLAIR, periependymal lesions along both lateral ventricles create the arch bridge appearance. Periependymal lesions follow the lining of the lateral, third, and fourth ventricles; hypothalamic involvement can cause narcolepsy, anorexia, and temperature dysregulation. The trident sign appears when a corticospinal tract lesion in the posterior limb of the internal capsule generates vasogenic oedema extending into the adjacent temporal lobe. Cortical lesions are a red flag — their presence should prompt reconsideration toward MS, MOGAD, or vasculitis.


Enhancement Patterns
NMOSD enhances in two characteristic ways that differ from MS: cloud-like enhancement (patchy, inhomogeneous, poorly defined margins) and pencil-thin periependymal enhancement (thin linear enhancement along the ventricular surfaces). Open-ring enhancement, by contrast, strongly favours MS — it is rarely seen in NMOSD.

title: NMOSD: The Astrocyte’s Revenge
section: The Three-Way Comparison: NMOSD vs MOGAD vs MS
text:
The Three-Way Comparison: NMOSD vs MOGAD vs MS









Feature
NMOSD (AQP4+)
MOGAD (MOG+)
MS




Optic nerve segment
Posterior + chiasm
Anterior, intraorbital
Anterior, intraorbital


Laterality
Bilateral
Bilateral
Unilateral


Length
Long (>½ nerve)
Long
Short


Chiasm
Commonly involved
Rarely involved
Rarely involved


Enhancement
Variable
Perineural (tram-track)
Variable


Spinal cord
LETM, central, cervicothoracic
LETM, H-sign, conus/thoracolumbar
Short, peripheral, cervical


Brain
Periependymal, corticospinal tract
Deep grey matter, cerebellum, ADEM-like

Prognosis
Poor, relapsing
Often monophasic, favourable
Variable



The perineural enhancement in MOGAD — a rim of gadolinium around the optic nerve sheath — reflects the accessibility of MOG on the outer myelin surface to antibodies in the surrounding orbital fat. Absent in NMOSD. That tram-track appearance on fat-saturated post-contrast sequences is one of the more distinctive signs in this entire group.

title: NMOSD: The Astrocyte’s Revenge
text:
Key DDx Mimics
Subacute combined degeneration (B12 deficiency): LETM with selective dorsal and lateral column involvement on axial cord imaging. No vasogenic oedema, minimal enhancement. Clinical context — B12 deficiency, sensory ataxia, peripheral neuropathy — is the key.
Acute spinal cord infarct: sudden onset, anterior spinal artery distribution. Classic axial appearance: “owl eyes” or “snake eyes” — bilateral anterior horn involvement. Restricted diffusion on DWI is the discriminator.
Susac syndrome: rare small-vessel disease with corpus callosum involvement at its central portion (not the ependymal surface as in NMOSD), producing small linear “snowball” lesions on FLAIR. The triad of sensorineural hearing loss, retinal artery occlusions, and encephalopathy is the clinical tell.
CLIPPERS: “peppering” gadolinium enhancement in the pons, middle cerebellar peduncles, and cerebellum. Steroid-responsive. The infratentorial predilection distinguishes it from NMOSD.
CMV ventriculitis: pencil-thin ependymal enhancement that closely mimics NMOSD periependymal patterns. The context makes the diagnosis: immunocompromised patient (HIV, transplant). Restricted diffusion and intraventricular debris support ventriculitis over NMOSD.
Sarcoidosis: cranial nerve and optic nerve thickening, nodular enhancement near the hypothalamus and visual pathways, leptomeningeal involvement. Known systemic sarcoidosis should always put this on the list.
GFAP astrocytopathy: shares longitudinally extensive myelitis, bilateral optic involvement, and periependymal enhancement with NMOSD. The discriminator is the enhancement pattern: GFAP produces radial perivascular enhancement perpendicular to the ventricles, while NMOSD produces periependymal or cloud-like enhancement. Optic involvement differs too — GFAP causes papillitis (disc oedema), NMOSD targets the posterior optic nerve and chiasm. CSF GFAP-IgG and AQP4-IgG serology are the definitive tests.

title: NMOSD: The Astrocyte’s Revenge
text:
The Big Picture
NMOSD lesions follow AQP4 — wherever astrocyte foot processes contact CSF or blood vessels, that’s where the disease attacks. Once that principle is internalised, the imaging map becomes predictable: posterior optic nerve and chiasm, long central cord lesions with bright spotty signs, periependymal brain lesions, area postrema. The key discriminators from MS come down to three things: location (posterior vs anterior optic nerve; central vs peripheral cord; periependymal vs periventricular brain), lesion character (LETM vs short segment; bright spotty signs vs Dawson fingers), and clinical context (bilateral optic neuritis plus myelitis; intractable hiccups as presenting symptom). When the distinction is unclear, AQP4-IgG serology is the decisive test.
